data.title: Wall Street Week Ahead
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Up for a challenge? Test your knowledge on the biggest events in the investing world over the past week. Take the latest Seeking Alpha News Quiz and see how you stack up against the competition.

Wall Street heads into a busy week dominated by major bank results. There will also be inflation data and a heavy slate of Federal Reserve commentary.

Earnings season kicks off with Goldman Sachs (GS) on Monday, followed by a wave of financial heavyweights on Tuesday, including JPMorgan Chase (JPM), Wells Fargo (WFC), Citigroup (C), and BlackRock (BLK). Later in the week, results from Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM), Netflix (NFLX), and PepsiCo (PEP) will offer insight into global demand, tech spending, and consumer trends.

On the macro front, the March Producer Price Index on Tuesday will be closely watched for signs of pass-through from recent oil volatility, while the Federal Reserve’s Beige Book on Wednesday will provide a snapshot of economic conditions across regions. A steady stream of Fed speakers throughout the week, including John Williams and Neel Kashkari, could also shape rate expectations.

Energy markets remain in focus with OPEC’s monthly report due Monday, while geopolitical developments continue to influence sentiment.

Earnings spotlight: Monday, April 13: Goldman Sachs (GS). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Tuesday, April 14: JPMorgan (JPM), J&J (JNJ), Wells Fargo (WFC), Citigroup (C). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Wednesday, April 15: ASML (ASML), Bank of America (BAC), Morgan Stanley (MS). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Thursday, April 16: Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM), Netflix (NFLX), PepsiCo (PEP). See the full earnings calendar.

In case you missed it

Citrini Research analyst gets first-hand look at Strait of Hormuz

Michael Burry buys JD, Alibaba shares; adds to Nvidia puts

Citi downgrades six software stocks as AI concerns intensify

The long and winding road for GTA 6 may be ending

SpaceX details IPO schedule

More departures at The Trade Desk as three execs depart

Investing Group Spotlight

After more than a decade on Seeking Alpha, Leo Nelissen has launched Main Street Alpha, an Investing Group focused on long-term, macro-driven investing. With over 50,000 followers, Leo’s approach blends macro insights, geopolitical analysis, and fundamental research to identify high-quality businesses and build disciplined, long-term portfolios.

Take a look at his latest Idea:

(Free Full Article) In this article, Leo Nelissen introduces two higher-risk stocks ahead of the launch of his Main Street Alpha service, highlighting opportunities beyond his usual conservative approach. The first, The Mosaic Company, operates in the agricultural fertilizer space. While historically a weak long-term performer due to its commodity nature, Leo views it as a strong inflation play, with improving supply-demand dynamics and rising prices potentially driving earnings growth and a stock rebound.

The second, Kinsale Capital Group, is a specialized insurer in the excess and surplus market. Leo considers it a long-term compounder, supported by strong cost efficiency, disciplined underwriting, and increasing use of technology like AI. Although facing short-term pressure from rising competition, its current valuation presents an attractive entry point.

Overall, Leo sees both stocks benefiting from a broader cyclical recovery, offering potential upside for investors willing to take on higher risk.

Main Street Alpha helps investors navigate markets with a macro-driven strategy, real-money portfolios, and a disciplined approach to capital allocation. Members get full access to institutional-quality research, three actively managed portfolios, timely trade alerts, and direct interaction with Leo Nelissen and his team. Join now to lock in early access pricing; the first 400 members can subscribe for just $499 per year before the price increases to $599. Learn more >>

What SA Analysts Are Watching

The Market Is Wrong About Claude Mythos: Buy Software, Buy It All

The Magnificent 7 Broke - That's Exactly Why I'm Buying

Netflix Earnings Preview: With The WBD Saga Over, Focus Shifts To Advertising And Live Sports

Taiwan Semiconductor Is Setting Up For Another Beat

JPMorgan Chase: Earnings Offer Catalyst Opportunity For Shares

Johnson & Johnson Q1 Earnings Preview: Time To Sell The Rally?

data.title: Ingles Markets' Surge Doesn't Mean Its Discount Is Gone
data.description:
The last few months have been an exceptional time for shareholders of Ingles Markets (IMKTA). Since I last reaffirmed the company as a ‘strong buy’ candidate back in January of this year, shares have skyrocketed 28.9%. That is significantly better than the 7.3% drop that the S&P 500 saw over the same window of time. The company has benefited from a surge in revenue, profits, and cash flows, even though it continues to struggle with some of its locations being closed down because of hurricane-related weather events. The market seems to finally be recognizing just how cheap the stock is. In fact, in my last article, I made the case that the discount that the business was trading at in relation to other similar firms would not last. And sure enough, I have been right so far.

The way I view it, some of the easiest upside has already been captured in recent months. But that doesn't mean that investors should sell. The company is much more favorable from a risk standpoint than other similar enterprises. It's also trading cheaper than most of them, especially when it comes to its price-to-book multiple. Long term, I expect performance to continue to impress. And because of that, I will maintain my ‘strong buy’ rating on the stock.

Still selling for cheap

The only new fundamental data that investors have regarding Ingles Markets covers the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. During that time, revenue for the company came in strong at $1.37 billion. That is up 6.6% over the $1.29 billion that the company reported a year earlier. This surge in revenue came in spite of the fact that the company suffered to the tune of $2.1 million from the fact that three of its locations remain closed as a result of Hurricane Helene, which decimated them back in September of 2024. The overall rise in revenue for the company, then, came from a surge in comparable store sales. That includes fuel. These revenues jumped $75.9 million year over year. That's an increase of 6.1%. But if we exclude fuel from the equation, the rise was even better at 6.2%. I wish I could say that we had more data on this to explain whether this was traffic, higher prices, or a mixture of the two. But unfortunately, management is quite opaque when it comes to reporting these figures.

Regardless, as revenue improved, profitability improved also. Net income shot up from $16.6 million to $28.1 million. Obviously, the higher revenue helped here. But that wasn't all. The firm saw its gross profit margin expand from 23.4% to 24.4%. Even without fuel costs, the company benefited to the tune of 76 basis points when it came to gross profit margin. Operating and administrative costs, meanwhile, declined from 21.8% of revenue to 21.5%. This dip was because of non-fuel items such as salaries and wages, insurance, bank charges, and more. As net profits improved, other profitability metrics for the company improved also. Operating cash flow, as an example, went from negative $43.6 million to positive $38.4 million. If we strip out changes in working capital, we get a rise from $45.2 million to $55.2 million. And finally, EBITDA for the company expanded from $50.4 million to $74.8 million.

One thing that certainly helped here was the fact that the hurricane related impact that the company saw is now a distant memory. Even though revenue continues to suffer because those stores remain closed, all of the costs associated with those closures have largely become a thing of the past. Even the revenue impact is small compared to what it was previously. Management estimated, for instance, that during a three-week window of time in 2025, road and power outages prevented several of its stores from operating, costing it between $55 million and $65 million in revenue. But seeing as how the impact for the most recent quarter was only $2.1 million, it's clear that the storm has passed.

One of the great things about the company, or perhaps the best thing about it, is the fact that shares are incredibly cheap. If we project out financial performance based on what we saw during the first quarter of 2026, we would get net profits for the year of $141.5 million. Adjusted operating cash flow would be $258.4 million, while EBITDA would come in at $357.5 million. Using these estimates, and also using the results that the company achieved for 2025, we can see in the chart above how shares are priced and how they stack up against five other similar enterprises. Focusing on the 2025 multiples, the firm is not the cheapest, but it's certainly not pricey either. Only one of the competitors is cheaper than it is on an EV-to-EBITDA basis. Two of them are cheaper on a price-to-operating cash flow basis, while three of them are cheaper in relation to earnings.

This is not the only lens through which we should view the business, though. In the chart above, as an example, you can see how the company looks from a price-to-book standpoint. This is perhaps the most striking valuation difference that I see. It is, by far, cheaper than these other comparable firms. In fact, if it were to trade at the price-to-book multiple of the cheapest of these competitors, that would imply upside for shareholders of 169.3%. And if it were to trade at the average, that would mean an upside of 412.3%. The price-to-sales multiple is not as impressive, but it's still quite impressive all the same. In the chart below, you can see that only one of the companies that I compared it to ended up being cheaper in this regard. All of the rest are more expensive, though not as expensive when it comes to the price-to-book approach.

Normally when you see a business trade at a significant discount like this, it's because of either declining financial performance, which we clearly do not see here, or high amounts of debt that make the company risky. That doesn't appear to be the case either. In the chart below, you can see the company's net leverage ratio compared to the net leverage ratio of its five competitors. None of them come even close. In fact, if Ingles Markets wanted to borrow enough money that it would have the same net leverage ratio as the lowest leveraged of the five competitors, that would mean it would have to grow its $152.1 million of net debt by another $270.4 million. This could certainly give the company fuel with which to grow further. But management has decided to remain principled in how they approach operations.

Takeaway

Based on all the data that is at our disposal right now, I continue to be shocked at just how cheap Ingles Markets happens to be. The company is insanely attractive from a valuation standpoint. That's especially true on a price-to-book and price-to-sales basis. The risk profile is low, and management has clearly demonstrated some strong performance in the most recent quarter. When you add all of this together, it makes for a very compelling ‘strong buy’ pick in my opinion.

data.title: Maple Leaf Foods: Re-Rated, But Upside Still On The Table
data.description:
One of my favorite candidates in the Canadian food space has been re-rated.

I'm talking about Maple Leaf Foods (MFI:CA), which, after slapping a 'Buy' rating, seems much more like the pure-CGP company they talked so much about after the spin-off from Canada Packers (CPKR:CA).

It's not that I expect them to become something like Saputo (SAP:CA), which has been my top pick for the space since April of last year, overnight.

They're still pouring a lot of money into capex for 'Fuel for Growth,' which ends up putting pressure on FCF in the short term.

But we can say that Maple Leaf is still in the early stages of what I told you it would become.

If you've just landed here and don't know the story yet, my take on Maple Leaf is that it's about to enter a phase of cash-cow-ification. Include everything you know about companies of this type, such as Premium Brands (PBH:CA) and Saputo itself:

  1. Fatter and more predictable margins (de-risk the spread business of the pork business and work with higher value-added branded products).
  2. EBITDA-to-OCF conversion likely grew up to over 90% (with Canada Packers embedded, it ranged between ~80 and 87%), making them more FCF rich.
  3. Distributions are also expected to accelerate (especially when investments decrease in ~3 years). They are boosting dividends for FY 2026 to $0.84 per share, 10.5% higher than last year and exceeding the average 5-year growth rate by 7.7%.
  4. Balance sheet much cleaner after the spin-off (net debt/LTM adjusted EBITDA fell from 2.7x to 2.1x).

In other words, this stock is becoming the type I most enjoy dissecting.

Good for me, good for you!

At this point you're probably saying, 'I already know all this, Pedro.' Fair enough. I think we both agree that some re-rating has happened in these four months, haven't we?

It turns out we've reached an interesting point for my thesis on Maple Leaf.

At ~$31, this stock is offering me a tiny upside to my more conservative price target of $33. And of course, I'm not forgetting that I'm expecting something close to a mid-to-high single-digit return.

Is it still a 'buy' here?

Well, before answering that, I want to give you a peek at the quarter. I think I can show you early innings of the transformation into a heavy-CPG company.

Some Things Are Already Shifting

The signs of re-rating are already out there. You just have to dig for them, and you'll find them.

At the top line, revenue was up ~8.1%, helped by strong sales in the U.S. and Canada. And if you go out, you might notice that protein is more important than ever on people's menus (especially Gen Z).

I'm a restaurant guy, and I can't help but notice that chains like Pollo Loco (LOCO) and Chipotle (CMG) launched their protein-added menus this year.

In other words, protein is on everyone's lips. Literally.

That backs up the pure-CPG thesis, but that's not the real story. Revenue should smooth out without pork's 'mini-cycles'.

But that's not where I'm going with this.

Adjusted EBITDA in Q4 FY 2025 jumped from $108.3 million to $117.3 million. It doesn't seem like much when you look at the margin (flat at 11.8%), but it gave a nice 21.7% bump when you look at the whole FY 2025.

Here's a peek at unadjusted EBITDA over the years:

There are basically three forces expanding EBITDA here.

One of them is 'Fuel for Growth' by cutting costs. In the earnings presentation, they make it very clear that they are optimizing the supply chain end-to-end.

But this looks like the biggest chunk came from the mix.

As I mentioned in the last article, shifting toward value-added poultry and branded product launches (see Musafir or Mighty Protein, for example) moves them away from the old spread-driven model and into more predictable margins.

You're probably wondering (quite rightly) if EBITDA increased, why did FCF shrink by just over 17% to $318.4 million in FY 2025?

It's important to provide some context first.

First of all, capex increased by almost $30 million to $126.3 million. Just normalizing that to FY 2024 levels would close the gap to almost $40 million.

Basically, they're opening two poultry plants (one in Ontario and another in London) and a 'Bacon Centre of Excellence' in Winnipeg. It looks like a push for better routing, some international reach, and a bit of R&D out of Winnipeg.

Who knows, maybe more branded products are on the way?

All of this contributes to the narrative of margin expansion.

The other drag is changes in operating assets.

Almost $50 million was absorbed from here in FY 2025 alone. It doesn't seem like a structural issue to me either. Other years (FY 2022, for example) saw larger drags, and some similar ones (FY 2021).

Anyway, the EBITDA-to-OCF ratio is around 91%. It looks very much like the long-term conversion I modeled earlier.

Even though it's smaller, this FCF of $318.4 million was already enough to cover dividends paid (including the special $0.60 per share), repurchase shares, and even repay some post-spin-off debt.

At the end of the quarter, they have $994.7 million in net LT debt. Significantly less than the $1.21 billion in FY 2024.

Adding it all up, you can see that:

  • The risk profile has decreased (and here I'm referring to both EBITDA volatility and the balance sheet).
  • They improved cash conversion by excluding tweaks to operating assets and one-off increases in capex.
  • Freed-up capacity will be used to distribute capital (either through buybacks or dividends).

What to Expect for Maple Leaf in FY 2026?

You're wrong if you think they maintained the guidance. They changed it, but only slightly, for FY 2026.

And I'll end the mystery here, since the biggest tweak is in revenue. When I wrote my first article (post-spin-off), management expected revenue to grow in the low single digits, leading me to model something close to $4.03 billion.

In the last earnings call, they projected a mid-single digit. So, I'll move the projection to ~$4.1 billion.

For adjusted EBITDA, they are still targeting $520 to $540 million. That is, a margin of something between 12.6% and 13.1%. See, it's still a bump from the 12.1% of FY 2025.

It's the same range I modeled in the last article.

Another angle I'd like to look at here is EPS.

Wall St. seems to be targeting $1.55 for FY 2026. This would be ~68% higher than the $0.92 they pulled last year.

Now, if they exhaust the NCIB (~7 million shares repurchased and retired, as I projected before), that would be 117.4 million shares outstanding and a net profit of ~$181 million based on these projections.

I prefer to stay out of this here. Net profit still seems quite volatile to me.

Using EBITDA makes more sense here.

Just to round out some projections, they are targeting a capex of $160 to $180 million. Much higher than the $126.3 million we saw last year.

It's obvious that FCF won't be prime this time.

For example, if I continue targeting ~90% conversion, the OCF would come in at ~$477 million at the midpoint of EBITDA and the FCF at somewhere between $297 and $317 million.

This would still be enough to pay the $104.5 million in dividends ($0.84 per share they've already announced), but if they want to exhaust the NCIB (they would spend just over $225 million at current prices), they would need to open up some of the cash they're sitting on or add some debt.

In that case, the cash deficit would be ~$32 million at the lower end of FCF. Net LT debt would slip to ~$1.02 billion, still well below the 3x adjusted EBITDA they're targeting in the long term.

Buy or Hold: What Fits Better Now?

I'm making a few small tweaks to my Maple Leaf valuation model.

For example, instead of targeting $1.4 billion in net debt (I had projected they would be at the 3x leverage limit with the cash they're sitting on), I'll now use that $1.02 billion after the cash burn or tick-up on debt.

On EBITDA, I'm looking at an unadjusted range of $500 to $520 million, staying conservative.

I can also use the range of 117.4 to 124.4, considering or not the buybacks.

So, enough talk, let's take a peek at the peers.

I'll use the same companies as before, such as Premium Brands, Conagra (CAG), and Hormel (HRL). But this time I'll add others like Cranswick (CRWCY) and Jamieson Wellness (JWEL:CA).

This gives me a multiple of 9.5x.

On the low end, approximately $500 million in EBITDA with no buybacks gets you to about $30. On the high end, approximately $520 million, and a fully used NCIB brings it closer to $33.40.

So, the short term already seems to be priced in here.

But what about looking a bit further out? Isn't there an upside there too?

Taking Wall St.'s projected revenue of $4.54 billion for FY 2028, plugging in an EBITDA margin of approximately 13% (EBITDA of approximately $590 million), and imagining something close to 115 million shares outstanding, the price target is close to approximately $35 (the same as Wall St.), discounting a cost of equity of 7.5%.

I could leave it here, but I'd be ignoring some of the metrics they shared at Investor Day.

I'll put the image here for you to take a look at:

This is gold for my model.

I'll use those $750 million in EBITDA, $2.2 billion in net LT debt (~3x EBITDA), and something between 100 and 110 million shares outstanding. I'll also discount to present value at ~7.5%.

That would give me something between $33.60 and $37 per share, depending on how many shares they repurchase.

Everything points to a price target in the mid-$30s for Maple Leaf.

Mathematically, at ~$31 the stock would have a price upside of almost 13%. Adding that to the mid-single-digit return from buybacks and 2.7% dividend yield, the total return here is almost 20%.

So I would consider a moderate 'Buy' here with a price target of ~$35 per share.

It's no longer an asymmetric setup. That re-rating already happened with the ~20% move, but there's still some room here, especially for income-lover investors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Simulations Plus: Down Substantially, Not An Automatic Buy
data.description:
Nearly two years ago, I called Simulations Plus (SLP) a steady growth player, yet I questioned the margins and its valuation. Down two-thirds ever since, the performance, let alone the relative performance, has been absolutely dreadful. This provides an opportune time to revisit the thesis, certainly, as the company just announced its second-quarter results.

While these results look solid, the full-year sales outlook (and certainly the earnings part of it) looks underwhelming. Consequently, shares have fallen to multi-year lows. In fact, shares trade at the lowest levels in about a decade, as investors fear stagnation and the rapid emergence of AI as a disruptive factor.

Valuations have come down a great deal, with real earnings and a strong balance sheet providing support. Valuations are not dirt cheap yet, as the AI overhang makes it difficult to build confidence into a constructive thesis here.

Other, higher conviction ideas, including recent M&A efforts, can be found at Value In Corporate Events.

About The Results

Simulations Plus reported relatively reasonable results at first sight. The company just announced an 8% increase in second-quarter sales to $24.3 million, with growth being pretty solid, split between software and services.

Operating profits of $5.6 million are actually quite solid, having doubled from the same period a year before and marking a dramatic improvement from the first-quarter results, which are seasonally very soft. Net earnings of $4.5 million were equal to $0.22 per share, with year-to-date profits reported at $0.26 per share; these are GAAP numbers.

For the year, revenues are seen as between flat and up 4%, translating into a $79-$82 million revenue guidance. With adjusted EBITDA margins seen around 28% of sales, adjusted earnings are seen between $0.75 and $0.85 per share. This is not too encouraging, as the company reported adjusted earnings of $1.03 per share for 2025. Not only are earnings seen coming down meaningfully, in the fall the company actually issued an original 2026 earnings outlook that called for earnings between $1.03 and $1.10 per share.

After adjusted earnings came in at $0.48 per share so far this year, that implies just a $0.32 per share earnings number seen in the second half of the year. The problem is that this excludes about $6 million in stock-based compensation expense on a pre-tax basis, which is equivalent to about $0.22 per share. If we subtract that, earnings realistically are seen around $0.58 per share. Comforting is that the company further reports a net cash position of $42 million, the equivalent of just over $2 per share.

Trading down to just below the $13 mark, the operating asset valuation stands around $10 and change per share, for a $200 million enterprise valuation. This values the business at around 2.5 times sales, and with realistic earnings at around $0.58 per share, operating assets trade at 20 times earnings, perhaps a bit lower.

Expectations Implode

Having last looked at the shares in the summer of 2024, shares still traded at $39 per share at the time. Investors were upbeat on the provider of software and consulting services in what is called the biosimulation market. In here, its solutions are used for drug discovery, development, research, and even regulatory submission processes, with the industry at large spending hundreds of billions each year on such items, as any increase in efficiencies results in big savings.

Having seen modest growth, the company was a rather sleepy business in the 2010s, as a $25 stock pre-pandemic jumped to the $80 mark during the pandemic, with investors hoping that the company could play a role in diagnostics. That optimism had already faded by the summer of 2024, when the stock was trading near the $40 mark, cut in half from the highs, and has ever since seen further pressure.

The reason for that is simple, as expectations were sky-high when the company announced a $100 million deal to acquire Pro-ficiency Holdings in 2024. Valued around $800 million, Simulations was valued at 10 times sales, based on pro forma revenues of $80 million, that latter number not having changed over the past two years. With such sales multiples, even high margins result in sky-high earnings multiples, and Simulations Plus did not necessarily have high margins, making me very cautious at the time.

What Now?

With revenues being flat over the past two years, it is clear that margins have been really sluggish. Adjusted earnings of $0.95 per share in 2024, rose to $1.03 per share in 2025 and are now seen at just around $0.80 per share. Moreover, realistic earnings are seen below $0.60 per share if we factor out stock-based compensation expenses.

By now shares have fallen to the lowest levels since 2017, with shares hitting the lowest levels in nearly a decade, as investors are clearly not buying into the growth story anymore, with margins pressured here. While the balance sheet is strong, the reality is that sustainable sales growth is modest, and while margins are good, the pace of progress, read growth, is simply not good enough.

This makes me cautious. On the one hand, a 10 times sales multiple and nosebleed-high earnings multiple back in 2024 have fallen to about 2.5 times sales and a near 20 times earnings multiple here. This is encouraging, and while second-quarter sales growth of 8% is encouraging, full-year growth is nonexistent. The reality is that Simulations Plus is not alone in this, with many diagnostic peers having seen dismal returns at large, and valuation multiples coming in a great deal.

All this makes me upbeat from a valuation perspective, yet the lack of growth makes me cautious as well, as either the industry is seeing tough times, or AI is having an impact on part of the business here. Of course, executives see this as a positive on the conference call, making the company likely more efficient, but clearly the market fears the disruption instead of the added efficiency here.

Now is not the time to get really bearish, given the past price action, from a risk-reward perspective, but that does not mean that this is a buying opportunity just yet here.

data.title: Micron Technology: I'm Buying And Not Looking Back
data.description:
Investment Thesis

They say that when it looks too good to be true, it probably is. I’ll argue that this isn’t the case with Micron Technology (MU) right now. The top and bottom lines have exploded, and Q3 2026 is looking to be a milestone for the company, not just because of revenue records, but because it will mean MU records more revenue in a 3-month period than it has ever done in a full 12-month period.

Concerns about this being a highly cyclical play are overblown, as by no means has AI momentum slowed down in terms of momentum, but then on top of this, companies will also have to replace its memory chips at a rapid pace to keep up with GPU improvements. This should help shore up both the top and bottom lines for MU. Based on this, MU is a Strong Buy at the moment for me. The valuation is too low to ignore, and the market has got it wrong about the future outlook of the business.

Stock Chart 5-Year Performance - A Comeback Story Like No Other

Over the past 5 years, the share price for MU has climbed at a rapid pace following incredibly strong earnings growth. It came largely due to rising revenues after a pretty harsh decline between 2022 and 2023. In 2023 MU announced it was planning to cut 10% of employees as a result of an oversupplied market and weak demand. The comeback story, though, was nothing short of incredible, as revenue has nearly doubled from 2022 levels. On top of this, MU is planning to hire upwards of 50,000 people as a result of the facility it's building in New York. This is a complete U-turn from what was happening a few years ago.

Margins have also recovered and surpassed historical highs, sitting at 41.49% net right now. What I find interesting, though, right now is how a company generating $24 billion in net income can still sit at a market cap of $475 billion, which comes out to a TTM P/E of 19.7x.

Our fiscal Q3 single-quarter revenue guidance exceeds the full year revenue for every year in our company’s history through fiscal 2024.

Looking at the guidance issued for this year though, or just the next quarter, it’s hard to argue why the stock is still trading so low. For reference, the revenue guidance is $33.5 billion in Q3 2026, with EPS at $19.15. Based on last quarter's diluted share count of 1.142 billion, that implies diluted earnings of $21.8 billion. If this is then annualized, it comes out to $87.7 billion. Suddenly the relatively moderate TTM P/E transforms to an FWD P/E of 5.41. I’d buy that any day of the week, but you already knew that because you read the intro and saw the rating I have for MU now.

The Three Product Pillars of Micron

I think what sets MU apart from other “traditional” AI plays like the ones making GPUs or CPUs is that it has full control of the supply chain, seeing as it owns the factories. Because these are so capital intensive to build, MU has an advantage here as it can supply a market overfilled with demand at the moment.

There are three main products that make up Micron right now, those being:

  • Dynamic Random Access Memory “DRAM”

  • Flash Memory “NAND”

  • High Bandwidth Memory “HBM”

DRAM is the short-term memory for a computer. If you have 50 tabs open in a browser and are running 5 different AI models as well, DRAM helps ensure that the data here is served at a rapid pace and without delay. NAND, on the other hand, is the long-term memory storage that MU makes. This is where comprehensive data is stored, not necessarily needed in an instance. When you turn off your computer or phone, this data type is still stored.

What has made MU grow its revenues rapidly in the past 12 months, and why it continues, is in large part because of the third product, HBM. This is a stacked version (like the floors in a skyscraper) of DRAM mostly used by high-end GPUs. Think of the ones that NVIDIA (NVDA) and its peers make. Historically, SK hynix has dominated this market, but MU’s new HBM3E chips consume about 30% lower power. In the data center market, lower power consumption means vastly higher profits.

I’m going to reference an article I wrote on NVDA back in December of last year. In there I discussed the evolution of the GPU performance and where it would logically be heading. As you might see, the performance (TFLOPS) and the memory bandwidth are rapidly increasing in a short period. Because of this, the current models are “starving” for data. HBM is the solution here as it serves more data, ensuring that a customer who runs a B200 chip actually gets the most out of it. But as the GPUs increase in speed and performance, so will the products that MU has to as well. This is where my replacement theory comes in for MU and why the market’s outlook consisting of declining earnings is a bit overexaggerated in my opinion.

Will Earnings Trickle Out?

At the moment analysts are expecting MU to post strong growth over the next few years, but in 2028 post a pretty substantial drop of 20%. Still, at 5.4x 2028 earnings, it's a bargain. But in the years after, the expectations continue for MU to post lower and lower earnings, reaching a 2030 FWD P/E of 14.4x. Again, not a rich valuation by any means. But it makes sense that MU trades at a risk premium if this is where the market sees the future earnings going.

Going back to my earlier argument about the need to replace these memory chips, I think it holds a lot of weight, as it will help shore up the revenues and earnings for MU, despite supply increasing and the potential for another 2023. With every new model that comes out from NVDA, there will have to be a new one from MU as well. With NVDA's next one, MU is developing its own, called HBM4.

To capture this, the two main competitors, SK hynix and Samsung Electronics (SSNLF), are both aggressively investing to raise capacity levels. SK has a plant in South Korea expected to be operational in 2027, ahead of schedule. The money poured in is some $90 billion.

It recently began commercial shipments of its 6th-generation HBM, HBM4, to key customers and plans to begin sampling custom HBM solutions in 2027.

Samsung, on the other hand, is retrofitting some of its existing factories to serve HBM demand. Construction is expected to start in Q3 this year and sampling in 2027. The likelihood is that actual shipments at volume would come out in 2028. This goes in line with the EPS estimates I shared earlier in an image above here.

So MU is up against companies with much, much more capital ready to be thrown at increasing production. Samsung has $87 billion in cash and short-term investments. SK holds about $25 billion, depending on the exchange rate between USD/KRW. For comparison, MU reported $13.9 billion in the last quarter.

To tackle this and maintain market share, MU has to rely on actually offering a superior product. I think it’s naive to assume prices won't decline as supply drops. But I also think the impact might not be as great as people think. Estimates put MU reporting $107 billion in revenues for 2028, a 30% YoY decline. If that is all, then I don’t see the bearish arguments holding much weight.

So in a head-on comparison with the main peers, it seems that the current models can hold their own against each other. Samsung boasts the highest speed; SK has better manufacturing capabilities. But MU offers the cheapest model to run because of the power efficiency.

One site puts the price of an HBM3e -8 hi at $1,800 right now. Notice that this is an older model than the ones in the table. Actual price for those is likely in the $3,000 - $4,000 range instead. Just before 2025 finished, SK also hiked their prices by 20%. If this continues, the price per unit could end up at $5,000-$6,000 before increased supplies hit the market.

We forecast an HBM TAM CAGR of approximately 40% through calendar 2028 from approximately $35 billion in 2025 to around $100 billion in 2028. This $100 billion HBM TAM milestone is now projected to arrive 2 years earlier than in our prior outlook.

In the Q1 FY2026 call held back in December, management guided for a pretty large market growth rate of 185% in a span of just 3 years. Assuming that current HBM3E prices are in the range of $4,000, then this implies 8.75 million units were shipped in the entire market.

Nvidia is expected to ship 5.9 million advanced graphics processing units (GPUS) this year, and between 6.9 million and 7.1 million units next year, he said.

This quote is quite interesting, I think. It implies that the 8.75 million unit figure I shared is a little on the rich side. NVDA has 94% of the market, meaning actual shipments should be in the range of 6.27 million. At that volume, the average price should have been around $5,500 ($35 billion / 6.27 unit shipments). The unit shipment growth rate in 2026 would be 18.7% based on the quote.

The main point of the table here is to visualize what happens when we see an oversupply in 2029/2030. I’m assuming that the HBM supply will outweigh the GPU shipments by 20%. By then attaching a 2.0x elasticity multiple, meaning 20% oversupply reduces prices by 40%, I can see that the pricing impact is considerable. In 2030 I’m assuming prices will slide an additional 10% as the market stabilizes somewhat after the supply shock. Ultimately I think this encapsulates the “doom and gloom” that the market is seeing for MU. But as I showcase below here, it’s not detrimental for MU in reality.

MU currently has 20% of the market, much of that gained because of Samsung losing its. I’m estimating that this will continue to climb slightly but then fall in 2029 and 2030 as SK and Samsung catch up.

The point here is that the revenue impact for MU at the peak to the bottom in 2030 is about $9.5 billion. For a company forecasted to generate over $100 billion this year, the impact is not that great, especially considering the cyclical nature of the memory market, which now looks more like a commodity than anything else. With the remaining product lineup that MU has, the top and bottom lines look well protected. The market expects a drop of 30% between 2028 and 2029. Based on my model here, I think it would be closer to 5-10%, assuming that MU reaches $155 billion in 2028. If price drops occur across the board, for DRAM and NAND as well, the impact might be closer to 25% instead. Still, this is not detrimental for MU.

Over the past 10 years, the net margin has been volatile but has, in more stable market environments, hovered around 30%. If this stays the same in 2029 and 2030, the net income would be around $34 billion. This is based on the $155 billion top-line estimate in 2028, declining by my worst-case scenario of 25%.

But in periods of oversupply, like in 2023, the net margin instead dropped to around 10%. Still, that doesn't discredit the buy case I have, as the valuation still looks moderate at 30x. Most large tech companies trade at this valuation. If the net margins don't decline that much either, the valuation looks even better. My fair value for MU is around the 30x earnings mark simply because of the market demand, its footprint, and how its product is lowering operating costs for end users, providing a MOAT.

Bear in mind as well that the revenue decline I’m modeling is 25%. If it’s less than that, this table would show MU trading at an even lower valuation. It’s a cyclical play for sure, but trading as it will never recover its bottom line. I’m quite familiar with the energy market, so I’m comfortable buying companies that might see a 20-30% drop in earnings between quarters and years simply because of supply and demand relations. The same will be true for MU over the long term, but as it continues to grow its capacity and maintain a dominant role, I think it presents a compelling investment case right now.

Risks

I think I’ve already covered most of the risks, or at least outlined them above. But to keep it brief, pricing and average selling price are the most important areas, I think. GPU shipments are likely to continue rising until 2030, given how much tech companies are investing in AI ventures. What will impact MU the most is the difference in HBM supply and GPU shipments. I’ve modeled for 20%, which is quite extreme. But if that gets surpassed, my models showing MU as a strong investment and even undervalued in certain scenarios would have to be revised. In short, the upside would be a lot lower, and there might be additional downside risk even.

The Full Picture

Micron Technology can be a divisive company to talk about. On one hand, you have bulls saying it's a once-in-a-lifetime opportunity right now. On the other side, you have bears talking about the inevitable supply disruption that will come once Samsung and SK’s factories come online. I think both can be true at once. MU trades at an attractive level now and still even after I’ve modeled out the supply disruption and impact it would have on pricing.

MU remains a highly competitive choice, and its valuation provides further upside, both now and in the long term. Rating shares a Strong Buy.

data.title: How Equity Income Can Cushion Inflation And Create Durable Returns
data.description:
By Snezhana Otto & Itay Moshenberg, CFA

Fears of an inflation spike bolster the case for adding equity income exposure to allocations.

Conflict in the Middle East has raised the specter of inflation again. As the energy price shock threatens to spread through the economy, it’s a good time to search for assets that can provide inflation resilience in portfolios. Dividend-paying stocks offer an effective hedge against inflation—as well as solid long-term return potential in other environments when actively sourced from the right parts of the market.

Rising oil and gas prices are classic mechanisms for a surge in inflation. Much will depend on the duration of the war and how long the Strait of Hormuz, a vital choke point through which a fifth of the world’s oil flows, stays shut. However, the situation may evolve, recent events have clearly reminded investors that inflation risks are far from extinguished.

Inflation had been on a declining slope since the consumer price index peaked at 9.0% in mid-2022 because of pandemic-related effects. Now, the risk of rising prices is again threatening growth, corporate earnings and equity returns, adding challenges to an investment playbook. So how can investors find assets that are likely to perform well in this changing environment but will also do well when inflation recedes?

Classic Inflation Hedges Are Flawed

Some investors might turn to “classic” inflation hedges, such as commodities. History suggests that these assets tend to perform well in inflationary eras. But when inflation retreats, investors might be left in the cold. Assets held for inflation protection, such as commodity stocks or gold, are not reliable sources of long-term returns. On the other hand, high-dividend stocks have delivered stronger risk-adjusted returns over time (Display).

High-Dividend Payers: Attractive Long-Term Return Potential, Correlated with Inflation

Dividend payers may not be top of mind for investors seeking high risk-adjusted returns because the last decade hasn’t been kind to them. Over seven of the last 10 years through 2025, the MSCI USA High Dividend Yield Index underperformed the S&P 500. High-dividend payers are often seen as old, stodgy companies, like utilities or food manufacturers, with limited growth potential. As investors flocked to high-flying technology and AI-driven growth companies, dividend stocks seemed like antiquated investment options.

Recently, we’ve seen signs that “old economy” stocks with attractive dividend yields are reawakening, and in the first quarter of 2026, dividend payers outperformed the S&P 500. While it’s too soon to say whether these trends signal a turning point, a long-term view provides perspective. When widening the lens, we found that stocks in the top two quintiles by dividend yield delivered superior returns over the past four decades. In our view, the second quintile is particularly attractive on a risk-adjusted basis, as it avoids riskier companies with unsustainably high payout ratios. And our research suggests that this long-term return potential comes with a bonus: attractive performance in an inflationary world.

High-Dividend Payers: Inflation Protection and Higher Risk-Adjusted Return Potential

Second-quintile dividend payers are among the rarefied ranks of asset classes that have positive beta to inflation (Display, above), based on our multidecade analysis going back to 1971. Dividend payers are good investments in inflationary times because they tend to have sustainable and relatively predictable cash flows. These cash flows are the lifeblood of payouts to investors and offer a stream of steady income to support returns—if they can be maintained.

That’s the rub.

Finding the right dividend payers always requires a thoughtful, active approach, but especially today. Dividends reflect a company’s ability to generate consistently high free cash flows to distribute to shareholders. Today, investors need to verify that cost inflation and growth pressures won’t squeeze margins and threaten dividend payouts.

How to Find Resilient Business Models Among Dividend Payers

Some business models will be advantaged as inflation rises. We believe companies with pricing power will be able to raise prices without dampening demand. Companies undergoing positive change, or those with sustainable competitive advantages, will also be well positioned to maintain or grow their dividends.

Examples can be found in different parts of the market. For example, auto parts and home improvement retailers operate in low-elasticity, less-discretionary corners of the retail industry and have historically demonstrated an ability to pass on inflation and tariff-related increases. Agricultural equipment manufacturers also enjoy pricing power by helping drive higher productivity for farmers through precision agriculture innovations.

In our view, it can be challenging to source the right dividend stocks with passive approaches. Equity income benchmarks gravitate toward the highest dividend payers, without regard for their ability to sustain those payments. Some investors might lean into a value benchmark as a proxy for dividend-paying companies, but these include many energy and financials companies with high volatility and lower return potential in non-inflationary environments.

High-dividend payers are a great tool to help investors navigate the inflation uncertainty we’re facing today. They can help offset the pain from rising inflation without the long-term performance penalty that typically comes with commodities or energy. Equity income portfolios that target higher-yielding companies, backed by businesses that can withstand pressure in tougher times, offer a dividend-driven defensive strategy for today’s new and evolving investing challenges.

The views expressed herein do not constitute research, investment advice or trade recommendations, do not necessarily represent the views of all AB portfolio-management teams and are subject to change over time.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Eastman Chemical: Went 'Buy' Too Early, But Now It's Cheap
data.description:
Sometimes you go into companies at a price that you later end up regretting quite a bit. Not because it's a bad company, per se, but because everything you do as a value investor depends on the value of the thing you're buying when you buy it. This is a relevant discussion because the company I am speaking of here, Eastman Chemicals (EMN), is not a bad company. It's just a company that's been under recent strain and pressure due to results and macro, which, unfortunately, has led it to underperform the market.

I bought shares in accordance with my latest recommendation, rating, and article for the business, which you can find here.

Eastman Chemicals is an attractive business from a fundamental perspective. A business could be little else when it has a BBB, IG-rated credit, and a dividend yield of about 4.5% that, by all appearances, is well-covered even during an earnings slump (which the company in 2025-2026 seems to be going through). So let's see what the company offers us, what we might do here.

EMN is a specialty chemicals company - not a commodity chemicals company. That means that the company's end-markets and producers focus on high-value and low-volume products (or formulations, in chemical vernacular) for highly specific applications. It insulates the company from broader commodity cycles but also increases the volatility when the specific specialties the company works with are in lower demand.

An example of a specialty chemical is something for pharma or an additive. An example of a commodity chemical, for comparison, is sulfuric acid. One is produced in bulk, the other in much lower quantities.

Eastman Chemical - Upside is Now Better, but Growth is Moderated

The drivers and what makes a company profitable in one versus the other are two entirely different things - in a commodity, it's all about scale and cost leadership, such as BASF (BASFY). I like commodity players as well as specialty players. In specialty, it's all about the R&D side, the service, and attractive patents.

What makes Eastman appealing is its leadership in many fields in sustainable materials, such as molecular recycling. It, over the years, has moved from a traditional supplier of chemicals to a specialty chemicals leader with high margins. It's not a unique move - many commodity players are looking to or have made similar moves because they want the advantages of either a combined operation or the high margins in the specialty field.

What really makes Eastman different is the specialization of molecular recycling to handle the problem of plastic waste. Eastman has some of the largest plants in the world for this - in TS and TX, as well as France. EMN is thus a global player in an attractive and forward-oriented field. These projects (which are not yet completely done) are expected to drive EBITDA and EPS growth going forward - though not until 2029.

The technologies they use for this, such as polyester and carbon renewal, are proven to break down complex plastics into materials completely similar to fossil-based things, which means that the output is "not dangerous" as plastics anymore - instead, virgin plastics, which do not degrade and can therefore be used more effectively.

Aside from its recycling ambitions, Eastman also has an attractive specialty materials operation. It has patents in Tritan (BPA-free material with consumer applications) and other advanced materials and functional products, including even textiles like Naia Renew, which, based on recent results, seems to be growing in fashion.

Recent results for the company show us the advantages of the company, in my view, and how it's being undervalued. This is despite the fact that 2025 was objectively a year of EPS decline.

EMN managed to generate in excess of $900M, close to a billion dollars, in OCF. This shows cash generation. It also exceeded its cost reduction plan; it achieved the operational goals for its recycling plant in TS with good production, and its products are gaining more and more traction. It also returned cash to shareholders using both dividends and buybacks.

Unfortunately, none of this could prevent an operating loss and EPS decline, as well as the general and macro-related price-cost trends. EBIT margin didn't just fall; it crashed by almost 700 bps YoY, and the lower sales driven by issues in inventory on the customer side also affected the company. Revenue was down 10-12% YoY for 4Q and 7-10% for the full year.

However, a small consideration here. The company's trends were not down due to ineffective or lower pricing. The company is getting the same money (almost) for the products it sells. And since much of the volume decline can be laid at the feet of macro trends, I view this as less significant than some analysts for the long-term company prospects. In fact, in the additives segment, the company managed to increase its revenues using pricing, which lends credence to the assumption that EMN is in a good position in its market.

For 2026, the company is ratcheting up its cost savings targets, as well as CapEx targets, now that much of the initial investment in its initiatives is finished. I also note that there were significant shutdowns of assets in 2025 - without those, results should be at the very least $30M higher just on the basis of that. The current OCF expectation for 2026 is in line with 2025. The current 1Q26 EPS, which I believe to be valid, is about $1.2/share on the high end, which, on the basis of 2026, comes to an AEPS forecast of about $5.7/share when accounting for the seasonal weighting of its annual EPS. This not only covers the $3.33/share dividend with a very high safety, because the company does not have much debt, but also risks for interest rate increases are lower than in other companies.

2025 was not a good year because Eastman is still waiting to see much of the upside from its operations that it started up. The company is underperforming due to poor trends in end markets it cannot itself affect - like automotive, construction, and industrial - coupled with significant destocking on the consumer inventory side.

The combination of cost reductions together with more contributions from recycling should slowly start to turn this around - which is also the expectation we currently have at about 5-7% AEPS growth for the 2026E period. The fact that even during one of the worst macro markets in a long time, the company still saw hundreds of millions in FCF is another reason for upside here.

The positive outlook for the first quarter in 2026 generally confirms this. With the company's balance sheet safe, I don't see much risk to the near term at this valuation. The strong cash flow (FCF) is enough in itself to cover the dividend, and debt is below 3.3x in terms of net debt/EBITDA.

Valuation for the Company - Some Near-Term Risks Remain, but Overall the Case is Strong

There are a few moving parts in the equation that justify attention from analysis. First of all, the company's 2026 Q1 and 2026 FY assumptions had a Brent crude price of about $70/bbl on the high end. As a feedstock, higher oil prices result in higher expenses. Obviously, the oil price is causing increased costs, but I would consider this to abate over the 2nd quarter of this year (I consider the war likely to dial down within 1-2 months).

The company has guided for further buybacks this year. I do not consider this to be an efficient use of capital at this particular time, given the company's valuation at about 13.5x normalized (P/E), which is already above the 20-year average of 12-13x.

In my last article, I gave the company a PT of $110/share. I am slightly lowering this to reflect the pressure of near-term uncertainties, to about $104/share. That means that if bought at below $104/share, there is an upside to a 12-14x P/E, which I consider to be a good approximation of the company's long-term value. I argue this growth (about 12-13% per year) is likely because of the company's operations in recycling coupled with a fundamental recovery in the more traditional sectors, such as specialty materials, additives, and fibers—with Fibers especially having a turnaround potential due to the low performance in 2025.

History has shown us that EMN tends to go up, then down. This reflects its underlying cyclicality and that of the sector overall.

Tendentially, it's a good idea to buy the company cheaply - and in this case, I believe the company is likely to see a longer-term growth trend exactly due to the recycling segment, which I believe has significant potential. Eastman is capturing large-scale demand from players with significant sustainability goals, given the capacity the company's assets have - well in excess of hundreds of millions of pounds per year.

The technology that Eastman uses not only does not even compete with other recyclers, but it also has a yield of over 90%, and the technique can handle the plastics that other methods can't touch - such as carpets, polyester-based textiles, and the like. The current facilities are just the flagship assets - the coming potential for expansion of this idea is in the billions, obviously.

This is what makes Eastman a player to count on, and why I believe the longer-term AEPS growth (2028-2032) may be currently considered conservative.

Using my estimate of about 13x P/E, I therefore see a 15%+ annualized rate of upside for the company at a price of below $104/share and would consider Eastman to qualify as a "BUY" here, even 2 years after the fact. It should be noted, though, that this is an investment that may take time to fully "mature", and therefore should be entered with this in mind.

Thesis

Eastman Chemical Company remains a very qualitative business with an upside going into the upcoming years. It also now comes at a superb valuation. It works to become completely ESG-neutral by 2050, and its pathway to the growth it is seeking has been mostly confirmed since 2026, despite problematic legacy end markets. I increase my P/E average target for the company to about 13x P/E, which comes to a PT of about $104/share because investing at that level, we can see 15% annualized towards that 13x P/E on a long-term basis, assuming the growth rates hold.

Risks for the company are limited to legacy end market demand, which is volatile, and execution for its ESG and recycling targets and goals, which may go up and down a bit, but I view this investment as one for the longer term. I may increase my position here going forward - though at my IG, Wolf of Value, I cover other companies with upsides that I consider either equal or better.

As of 2026, I am reiterating "BUY" for Eastman Chemicals.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them.

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. Based on this and a price target of $104/share, where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me, I give the company a "Buy" rating as of April 2026. The company fulfills almost all of my fundamental criteria for investment.

data.title: Industry Outlook: Visible Alpha Breakdown Of U.S. Banks' First Quarter 2026 Earnings Expectations
data.description:
US large-cap banks kick off first quarter earnings season on Tuesday, April 14, with JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM), Citigroup Inc. (C), and Wells Fargo & Co. (WFC) reporting first, followed by Bank of America Corp. (BAC) on Wednesday.

The big four entered 2026 with strong momentum following a record 2025, supported by resilient economic activity, moderating inflation, and still-elevated interest rates. Markets have remained constructive but volatile, with rate-cut expectations shifting and capital markets activity gradually improving from late-2025 lows. Against this backdrop, Visible Alpha consensus expectations point to steady top-line growth, modest profitability gains, and early signs of normalization in credit costs.

Across the group, revenues are expected to grow in the mid-to-high single digits year-over-year, led by JPMorgan (+7.3%), Citi (+8.3%), Wells Fargo (+7.5%), and Bank of America (+5.7%). This growth is driven by continued strength in trading and investment banking pipelines, which began recovering in Q4 2025, stable loan growth, particularly in consumer and commercial segments, and elevated (though plateauing) net interest income (NII) levels.

Profitability is expected to improve, with EPS growth ranging from 7% at JPMorgan to over 30% at Citi, the latter benefiting from restructuring progress and operating leverage. ROE trends are expected to be mixed, improving at JPMorgan, Bank of America, and Citi, but declining at Wells Fargo. While efficiency ratios show improvement at JPMorgan and Bank of America and deterioration at Citi and Wells Fargo, reflecting ongoing investment spend and restructuring costs.

Net interest margins face incremental pressure

Despite solid revenue growth, net interest margins (NIMs) are expected to show mixed-to-declining trends. Bank of America, Citi, and Wells Fargo are all expected to see sequential NIM compression, while JPMorgan stands out with a modest NIM uptick. Banks have flagged that the peak benefit from higher rates is behind them, and Q1 expectations reinforce that trend. Still, NII remains resilient, growing across all banks.

Balance sheet growth remains intact

Loan and deposit growth is expected to remain broadly positive in Q1. Total assets, deposits, and loans are seen increasing across banks. The most notable trend into Q1 is continued strength in loan growth. This builds on Q4 momentum, where banks pointed to healthy consumer balance sheets and steady borrowing activity, alongside improving corporate demand tied to a rebound in dealmaking and investment banking pipelines.

Stable fundamentals support cautious normalization

A key area of focus this quarter will be credit normalization. On an aggregate level, provisions for loan losses are expected to decline modestly, driven primarily by reductions at JPMorgan (-7.5% YOY) and Citi (-4%), partially offset by a notable increase at Wells Fargo (+22.6%) and a broadly flat trend at Bank of America (+0.8%).

Importantly, non-performing asset (NPA) ratios remain low, generally below 1%. So, while some banks are building reserves cautiously, actual credit deterioration remains contained.

Capital ratios show a mixed picture. JPMorgan and Citi are expected to strengthen CET1 ratios in Q1, while Bank of America and Wells Fargo are expected to see slight declines. This comes amid continued uncertainty around Basel III endgame regulations, which banks have been preparing for since late 2025. Capital return expectations (buybacks/dividends) will remain a key discussion point on earnings calls.

Overall, Q1 2026 is expected to shape up to be a solid quarter for large US banks. Revenue growth is projected to remain supported by resilient lending activity and improving market conditions, while margin pressures and elevated costs continue to weigh on operating leverage. At the same time, credit quality is seen remaining stable and capital levels strong, providing a solid foundation as banks navigate an evolving macro and regulatory environment.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: THD: Thailand Stocks Defy Bearish News In Early 2026, But Too Early To Buy The Dip
data.description:
iShares MSCI Thailand ETF overview

The iShares MSCI Thailand ETF (THD) is a very broad-based ETF offering exposure to Thailand’s stock market. It has a long history dating back to 2008; however, it has produced poor performance. According to its latest factsheet, it held over 80 different securities, with only one stock having a weight of over 10% of the portfolio. The top ten securities account for 57% of the portfolio, and the largest sector weight is currently the 14% exposure to the energy sector.

I last covered THD in August of 2024, which was, as we have become accustomed to, an interesting time in Thai politics. Back then I did see some green shoots in terms of how such political moves may potentially affect their stock market. I also viewed the historic poor returns as providing a cheap valuation backdrop where a contrarian buying opportunity may eventuate.

Since that time, almost a couple of years ago now, Thai stocks have been very volatile. In the context of even more eventful times in Thai politics over the last year, I thought it was an opportune time to take another look at their stock market.

I still find the valuation story and the contrarian aspect to owning Thailand stocks now tempting. The news flow is full of more gloom than previously in terms of the outlook for the Thai economy and stock market. At the same time, though, dips in their market have been getting shallower. This setup can often be a bullish one, as it indicates perhaps any potential sellers of Thailand equities have had enough reasons to act already.

iShares MSCI Thailand ETF key facts

Are Thai stocks climbing a wall of worry?

In late 2024, I referred to various Thailand economic policy discussions that were an encouraging sign the country was open-minded to actively boost economic growth.

In this respect, 2025 was generally plagued by ongoing uncertainty on many of these initiatives. Such uncertainty now, though, is increasingly looking to now be in the rearview mirror.

Some examples include:

  • Thailand’s “Digital Wallet” scheme—This cash handout scheme was widely criticized by economists. Some cynics viewed it as primarily a data-capturing exercise. It began with moderate impact and shortly after was shelved so the funding could instead be directed to other areas.
  • Government measures to boost the stock market—Significant money was raised for a new Thai ESG fund launched in April last year by offering tax incentives. This followed the success of the Vayupak Fund, a Thai state fund that raised US$4.5 billion and helped stabilize their equity market in late 2024.
  • Visa reform to boost tourism—In July of 2024, Thailand announced longer visa stays to boost their economy. At the time there were debates about whether such measures were too relaxed. It was not long before these changes were linked to more issues with mule bank accounts and a crackdown involving bank account freezes. Since then, the new Destination Thailand Visa (DTV) has been scrutinized far more heavily. The more relaxed 60-day visa-free stay for many tourists is now set to revert to the old 30-day visa-free period.
  • Thailand joining BRICS—While economists also debated the negative impacts of Thailand joining BRICS, the country was able to navigate the related worries about its trade relationship with the U.S. After the Thailand stock market initially fell heavily on news of new tariff measures threatened by the Trump administration on “Liberation Day,” negotiations were quick to take place. Thailand, along with most of Southeast Asia, soon finalized new lower tariff rates.

If there is one thing stock markets generally hate, it is any sort of uncertainty. Putting aside one's views on the effectiveness of various measures discussed above, at least there looks to be a clearer path forward on such policies.

Recent Thailand election stock market implications

Another major uncertainty Thailand had to deal with recently is the election held in February this year. This was particularly weighing on the minds of stock market investors, given Thailand's political history involving instability and regular military coups.

The Thailand 2026 election result came as a surprise. Looking at it purely from a stock market investor’s perspective, the result is positive. It provides a more certain path forward for various reasons.

To start with, it was a clear win that avoided the potential for the result to be debated for weeks or months into the future. The result suggested Thailand prioritized stability over reform. While one could argue some major economic reform is necessary, a new government tackling such reform is often something stock market investors would prefer to steer clear of. Investors typically rather adopt a “better the devil you know” mindset when it comes to government intervention.

To illustrate the above, without wanting to read too much into short-term stock market sentiment, given the surprise election result, it is interesting to observe what occurred after that weekend. The Monday after the election result, Thai markets surged by 3.5 percent. – “In a sign of optimism, global funds bought the most Thai stocks in four years on Monday (Feb 9)”.

Thailand's tourism sector outlook is admittedly poor

One key concern is the worrying outlook for Thailand’s tourism sector, which can’t seem to take a trick right now over the last year or two. Various factors out of left field have not helped.

Early in 2025, a Chinese actor was abducted from Thailand, and the publicity this generated was significant. To quote from the previously linked article, “The publicity around Wang’s case couldn’t have come at a worse time for Thai tourist operators, who had been expecting a surge of arrivals during the Lunar New Year holiday, China’s peak travel season. But there are signs Chinese tourists are looking elsewhere. Over the weekend, flight cancellations from China to Thailand surged 150%, Chinese state media reported, citing data from travel analytics firm ForwardKeys.”

As 2025 progressed, the relaxed attitude on the changes to visas announced in mid-2024 seemed to have changed. The media was quick to jump on the possible links between such relaxed visa policies and other matters. A potential connection was suggested to abductions and crime syndicates, as mentioned in the previous article, and the crackdown I touched on earlier with freezing many Thai bank accounts.

Adding to the headwinds for the tourism sector was a stronger Thai baht than most had expected. This occurred whilst there were mixed messages about how committed Thailand was to visas like the DTV and the recent visa-free period expansion to 60 days for many countries.

We also had major escalations in tensions between Thailand and Cambodia. This saw widespread deadly clashes along the border, particularly worrying during July of 2025 and again later that year in December.

Now tourism is interrupted by the Iran war. Dubai is a common transit hub for tourists entering Thailand that holidaymakers would now want to avoid. Then there is the obvious fact of elevated airfares and other cost inflation issues as a result of this conflict.

As I write this, it is days away from the Thailand New Year, and what typically follows is “low season” for tourism. This is why I expect no relief over the next few months at least regarding the news flow surrounding Thailand’s bleak tourism sector prospects.

That being said, as I suggested earlier, many of these issues above could be considered “left field” in nature. The fundamental attractions of Thailand as a tourist destination have not disappeared. In fact, maybe in the medium term it can appeal to the Dubai social media influencer types who might now be reconsidering where to make their home base.

The likely short-term negative news flow in upcoming months could pave the way for a buying opportunity in Thai stocks.

Thai stocks significantly underperformed in 2025

Emerging market equities in general had a very good 2025, finally outperforming the U.S. One notable exception, however, was Thailand (+4%). Their stock market also performed very poorly last year versus some regional peer ETFs such as Malaysia (+17%), Vietnam (+65%), and Singapore (32%).

iShares MSCI Thailand ETF long term performance

The long-term performance has been very disappointing, although momentum has shifted in the last 12-month period.

Thailand stock market valuations in 2026 after Q1

The underperformance of Thai stocks in 2025 depressed valuations to such an extent that it has assisted in providing a backdrop of strong gains thus far in 2026. In Q1 the Stock Exchange of Thailand Index, SET50, gained almost 15%.

Despite this very strong start in 2026, Thailand still screened as the 6th cheapest market in a list of 43 countries in the world, according to work from Meb Faber's The Idea Farm, as shown here.

One can refer to the previous link to see how this analysis is based off quarterly CAPE ratio updates as of March 31, 2026. It also combines a range of other quantitative valuation variables.

It is worth noting in the above context how the Thailand market might be disadvantaged in such comparisons to peers due to its lower weighting of financials. Bank stocks have a tendency to trade cheaply on measures such as P/E and P/B. This might bias other equity markets with high exposures to banks to look much cheaper. For example, in markets such as Indonesia, Singapore, and Malaysia, you will find their ETFs have circa 40 to 50% exposure to the financial sector. The Thailand ETF, in comparison, sits at just a 13% weight.

We now have a rare setup where the Thailand stock market appears to have both valuations and momentum in its favor.

iShares MSCI Thailand ETF Top Holdings and Sectors

iShares MSCI Thailand ETF portfolio characteristics

Weak economic outlook for Thailand in 2026, but do stock market investors care?

To be clear, Thailand is not the stock market for those that believe that strong economic growth is a prerequisite for a country's equities to produce good returns.

I would like to point out, though, that looking backwards at last year's GDP or the country's historical returns over the last decade is not always a good approach. An interesting reminder of this just last year in emerging markets was the performance of the stock markets of India vs. South Korea. Stocks did ok in India last year, rising around 10%, with their GDP growth sitting impressively around 7%. South Korea's GDP, on the other hand, was much lower at around 2%. Yet after their stock market performed poorly for many years, it finally surged circa 80% last year.

One factor helping South Korean stocks last year was a government initiative specifically aimed at helping their stock market known as the Value-Up Program. Hence it is why I felt it was worth mentioning earlier some recent government initiatives in Thailand, such as the Vayupak Fund and new Thai ESG funds.

It is helpful when a country prioritizes its stock market. Many investors also view the good recent run in Japanese equities as at least partially helped by their government introducing corporate reforms. Japan's GDP growth has been quite low the last few years, even as their stock market has surged.

One should fully expect that Thailand will record sluggish economic growth this year. According to the research from the Asia Development Bank shown here, Thailand is on course to record the slowest growth in ASEAN, with their forecast at just 1.8%.

Risks

In ADB's economic forecasts mentioned above, they highlight some factors I already discussed earlier. These prominently include mounting pressure from softer tourism and high energy costs as a fallout from the Middle East conflict.

I made the point earlier that such factors have the potential to be more temporary in nature and thus could provide a potential dip in Thai stocks to consider buying.

The bigger, longer-term risks I see with the Thailand stock market are more structural in nature. To quote directly from the ADB research, "ADB also pointed to the burden of household debt as a major domestic constraint. Private consumption has remained soft as families continue to face heavy debt obligations, limiting spending power at a time when higher energy and logistics costs are feeding through the economy."

Conclusion

I have approached my latest look at the Thailand stock market with a mindset that a buying opportunity may emerge. This is primarily due to cheap valuations and it feeling like one of the more "hated" markets I have seen in recent years. The news is pessimistic, yet such news is usually so widely known and discussed I wonder whether the selling of this market might have become exhausted last year.

I do not yet have any meaningful exposure to Thai equities, though, and it is possible this opportunity was more of one to act on late last year.

With the election result going the way it did, though, and the market pulling back from the highs a few weeks ago, I wanted to take a closer look again.

Due to the Iran conflict, though, it is anyone's guess where the Thailand SET Index might settle in even a week from now. Just as I was getting a little bit tempted to buy the dip after a 10% correction, as I write this on the weekend of April 11th, it has recovered much of this already.

I would therefore hold off buying THD for now. The news flow may well get worse before it gets any better. I do see it as encouraging, however, how the market bounced in mid-last year and was very resilient amidst negative headlines throughout. I therefore view any future potential correction to retest where we began the year at around the 1,250 level on the SET Index as a dip to buy. This likely equates to approximately the low $60 range for this THD ETF.

data.title: Haverty Furniture: Idiosyncratic, Cash Yield Income Play But Not Best-In-Class Value
data.description:
Haverty Furniture Companies, Inc. (HVT) is a specialty furniture retailer serving the suburban US market through 129 stores, so it has a mid-market footprint, reflective also of its mid-to-small cap market cap of around $370 million when considering class A into common stock conversions. They are also, therefore, still in a growth tenor, targeting 5 openings of stores, usually in big box real estate, per year in the Southeastern US in affluent markets. They try to do a more tailored model, giving free consultations to buyers, who then typically make significantly larger purchases (double), likely reflecting winning wallet share through that sort of in-store, almost interior design engagement. At this scale, it reflects an effective competitive lever. The normalized FCFY is close to 10%, we think, once 2026 expansion step-ups are passed, and also given the fact that tariffs may be firmly deemed illegal. Also, additional tariffs of 5% on wood furniture hadn't been put into place in time before the Supreme Court ruling. But in general, the company has mitigated tariff exposure thanks to a strong upholstery business where import intensity is lower, and also as demonstrated in the results where China exposure was reduced to below 5% and direct imports generally limited.

The strong FCFY, which we believe is the main draw here, easily supports the around 6% dividend yield currently and also continued growth, making Haverty an attractive income play. They have around $177 million in real estate, and net of the net financial position driven by leases, that covers around a third of the market cap. However, the real estate and value angle, in our view, remain sharper at our high-conviction furniture play, Leon's Furniture (LNF:CA), which also has FCFYs around 10% currently. So they win out. Moreover, there are clearly risks with respect to reinflation and how that could affect discretionary purchases on the front line as a consequence of demand destruction. Nonetheless, Haverty is a solid income play with a decent FCFY proposition. It's just not the optimal play within our coverage universe in our view.

Business and Results

They are proposing significantly higher CAPEX in 2026 compared to 2025, where cash flow run rates from operations have been between $50 million and $60 million in the last two years. FCF will be halved, it looks like, into 2026 in order to drive growth. Most of the FCF will be going into dividends, which look just about sustainable.

We're impressed by the cash flow generation.
